options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly as a single stat dose
Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly weekly for three doses
Procaine penicillin 1.5 g intramuscularly once daily for 10 days
Ceftriaxone 1 g intramuscularly once daily for 10 days total
Acute viral hepatitis A infection
Acute calculous cholecystitis with ascending cholangitis
Tubo-ovarian abscess with subdiaphragmatic spread
Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
Fitz-Hugh-Curtis perihepatitis (gonococcal or chlamydial)
Postural hypotension on arrival with dry mucous membranes on examination
Creatinine has more than doubled from her three-month baseline value
Urine sodium 8 mmol/L with FENa under 1% and concentrated urine
Hypernatraemia (sodium 148 mmol/L) reflecting a free water deficit clinically
Absence of hydronephrosis on bedside renal tract ultrasound today
Normal serum complement C3 and C4 on initial screening bloods
Mildly elevated blood pressure (138/86 mmHg) on examination today
Trace haematuria on dipstick without dysmorphic red cells on microscopy
Heavy proteinuria (UPCR 480 mg/mmol) with serum albumin 22 g/L
Negative ANA and ANCA serology on the secondary cause screen
IV 3% hypertonic saline 150 mL boluses, capping 24-hour rise at 8-10 mmol/L
IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 250 mL/h with hourly sodium measurement
Free water restriction to 800 mL daily as the sole acute intervention
Oral tolvaptan 15 mg now to block V2 receptors and aquarese free water
Allow spontaneous correction by treating the underlying cause over 24 hours
Continue subcutaneous insulin pump unchanged with 4-hourly capillary glucose monitoring
Switch to variable-rate IV insulin infusion targeting CBG 4-7 mmol/L
Stop all insulin in labour and recommence after delivery of the placenta
Aim for capillary glucose 8-10 mmol/L to reduce maternal hypoglycaemia risk
Give a single dose of long-acting insulin glargine 20 units now
Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily orally for 7 days post-discharge
Mechanical thromboprophylaxis with TED stockings alone for duration of admission
Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily for 6 weeks postpartum
Prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for at least 10 days postoperatively
Aspirin 100 mg orally daily for 6 weeks postpartum as VTE prophylaxis
Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation and exclude footling breech presentation
Elevate the presenting part manually and instil 500-700 mL of saline into the bladder via catheter
Administer a second dose of subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg and place the woman in Trendelenburg position
Prompt the woman to push actively to deliver the foetus as quickly as possible vaginally
Attempt manual replacement of the prolapsed cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV for 24 hours after delivery or last seizure
Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 5 minutes, then 2 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours postpartum
Magnesium sulphate 4 g IM loading dose into each buttock (8 g total), then 5 g IM 4-hourly for 24 hours
Magnesium sulphate 2 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours after the last seizure
Magnesium sulphate 6 g IV loading dose over 30 minutes, then 1.5 g/hour IV infusion for 48 hours postpartum
She may attempt pregnancy once hCG becomes undetectable, with no increased risk in subsequent pregnancies and no surveillance needed
Avoid pregnancy for at least 6 months after hCG normalises, use reliable contraception, with monthly hCG surveillance throughout that period
She should undergo a hysterectomy because complete moles have a 15-20% risk of progression to choriocarcinoma and future pregnancy is contraindicated
She requires a single dose of intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m² immediately to prevent progression to persistent trophoblastic disease at any stage
She should undergo CT chest and abdomen to stage the disease before pregnancy, as complete moles frequently metastasise to the lungs
Anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, plus pelvic ultrasound for anomalies
Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotypes, inherited thrombophilia screen, and outpatient diagnostic hysteroscopy
Full blood count, thyroid function tests, fasting glucose, and 75-gram oral glucose tolerance test
Serum anti-Müllerian hormone, early follicular FSH, oestradiol level, and ovarian antral follicle count
MTHFR mutation testing, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, and fasting homocysteine level
Refer to a women's health physiotherapist for pelvic girdle exercises, support belt fitting, and analgesia
Induction of labour at 37 weeks gestation to minimise further musculoskeletal damage and ongoing maternal discomfort
Strict bed rest for the remainder of the pregnancy with bedside toileting and meals provided
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the symphysis pubis joint under image guidance in interventional radiology
Prescribe regular oral opioid analgesia such as immediate-release oxycodone for severe ongoing pelvic pain
Red degeneration of fibroid; manage with paracetamol or codeine, rest, and monitor foetal wellbeing
Placental abruption; arrange immediate delivery by category 1 emergency caesarean section now
Ovarian torsion; arrange urgent emergency diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion and possible cystectomy
Acute cholecystitis; arrange abdominal ultrasound with gallbladder assessment and surgical review
Preterm labour; commence nifedipine tocolysis and intramuscular betamethasone for foetal lung maturation
Communicate empathetically about the loss, discuss induction versus expectant delivery, and offer bereavement support with cause investigations
Arrange immediate category 1 emergency caesarean section to deliver the foetus without further discussion or counselling
Discharge home with a plan to return spontaneously for routine vaginal delivery at 40 weeks gestation
Administer two doses of intramuscular betamethasone immediately to aid foetal lung maturation before delivery
Order a continuous CTG over thirty minutes to confirm the absence of foetal heart activity
Concealed placental abruption with retroplacental clot
Placenta praevia with painless antepartum bleeding
Uterine rupture along a previous caesarean scar
Labour dystocia from cephalopelvic disproportion now
Chorioamnionitis with secondary maternal sepsis present
Refer for psychological therapy (CBT or interpersonal therapy) and consider sertraline 50 mg daily, with risk-benefit discussion in breastfeeding
Reassurance that the 'baby blues' are normal and will resolve without any intervention or follow-up within a few weeks
Immediate admission to a mother-baby psychiatric unit due to perceived risk of harm to the baby in the home setting
Commence fluoxetine 20 mg daily and advise her to stop breastfeeding due to neonatal serotonergic risk concerns
Advise increased exercise and structured social engagement only, with reassessment in routine clinic in four weeks time
Arrange immediate delivery by category 1 emergency caesarean section under general anaesthesia now
Admit for observation, administer betamethasone, and plan elective caesarean at 37-38 weeks if stable
Induction of labour with artificial rupture of membranes and intravenous oxytocin titrated to contractions
Discharge home on strict bed rest with verbal instructions to return if any bleeding recurs
Perform a digital vaginal examination immediately to assess cervical dilation and effacement at the bedside
Attempt the Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre despite the epidural, rolling her onto hands and knees with assistance
Perform the Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement and immediate emergency caesarean section delivery
Apply firm sustained fundal pressure to dislodge the impacted anterior shoulder against the symphysis
Perform a generous episiotomy and immediately reattempt the McRoberts position with suprapubic pressure
Proceed directly to symphysiotomy with local anaesthesia to increase the available pelvic diameters
Low-dose aspirin 100-150 mg daily plus prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily throughout pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum
Low-dose aspirin monotherapy, as she has no thrombosis history and heparin increases bleeding without proven additional benefit
Warfarin 5 mg daily from the second trimester after organogenesis, with bridging LMWH cover in the first trimester
Low molecular weight heparin alone throughout pregnancy, as aspirin has not improved outcomes in obstetric APS clearly
Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg infusions monthly throughout pregnancy as the most effective treatment regimen available
Take blood cultures, give IV ampicillin, gentamicin, and clindamycin immediately, and resuscitate with 30 mL/kg crystalloid
Perform an immediate category 1 emergency caesarean section to deliver the foetus and achieve infectious source control
Transfer to the intensive care unit for invasive central venous pressure monitoring before starting any fluid resuscitation
Administer intravenous paracetamol 1 g over 15 minutes to reduce the maternal fever and then clinically reassess
Order an urgent CT abdomen and pelvis to exclude an intra-abdominal source before commencing any empirical antibiotics
Total abdominal hysterectomy with placenta left in situ, with internal iliac artery ligation or pelvic packing if bleeding persists
Attempt piecemeal manual removal of the adherent placenta in theatre to gain control of the bleeding
Close the uterus with placenta in situ and transfer for emergency uterine artery embolisation in interventional radiology
Apply a B-Lynch compression suture and intrauterine balloon tamponade with gauze packing if needed
Administer intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g and recombinant factor VIIa as definitive medical management here
Call category 1 emergency caesarean, activate massive transfusion protocol, and prepare for possible hysterectomy for suspected rupture
Administer terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneously to relax the uterus and improve foetal perfusion, while preparing for caesarean section
Perform an urgent speculum examination and foetal fibronectin test to confirm membrane rupture before proceeding to any surgery
Apply firm sustained fundal pressure to expedite vaginal delivery, as the presenting part has already descended into the pelvis
Commence an intravenous oxytocin infusion at 10 mU/min to augment contractions and achieve a rapid vaginal delivery now
Maternal oral digoxin 0.5 mg twice daily for 2 days then 0.25 mg twice daily, with weekly foetal echocardiography
Maternal oral sotalol 80 mg twice daily with serial maternal ECG and foetal heart rate monitoring
Maternal oral flecainide 100 mg twice daily with weekly maternal ECG and foetal rhythm surveillance
Direct foetal intramuscular digoxin injection via cordocentesis under continuous ultrasound guidance in a tertiary centre
Emergency category 2 caesarean delivery at 30 weeks gestation to manage the foetal tachycardia ex utero
Intravenous thiamine 500 mg three times daily for 2-3 days, then 250 mg daily, to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Oral folic acid 5 mg once daily for the duration of the hyperemesis admission today
Intravenous vitamin K 10 mg slowly over 30 minutes prior to commencing any dextrose containing fluids
Intramuscular vitamin B12 (hydroxocobalamin) 1000 mcg as a single dose now before glucose replacement
No specific vitamin supplementation is needed before commencing dextrose containing intravenous fluid replacement therapy
Intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m² as a single dose, with uterine artery embolisation arranged within 24 hours
Proceed urgently to immediate emergency hysterectomy under general anaesthesia in the operating theatre now
Administer oral mifepristone 200 mg daily for 7 days to induce placental necrosis and resorption
Observe without any adjuvant therapy and monitor with serial transvaginal ultrasound and beta-hCG measurements
Commence a continuous intravenous oxytocin infusion at standard postpartum dose for 24 hours total
Emergency McDonald cerclage, indomethacin 50 mg PR then 25 mg PO 4-hourly for 48 hours, plus betamethasone two doses
Administer betamethasone with strict bed rest only, as cerclage is contraindicated when membranes are visibly bulging through the os
Emergency cerclage and betamethasone, but avoid indomethacin tocolysis due to risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure in foetus
Induce labour now and counsel that the pregnancy is non-viable at 21+5 weeks gestation with poor outcomes
Apply a transabdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage under general anaesthesia and commence intravenous magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection
Planned caesarean at 35-36 weeks by a multidisciplinary team with internal iliac balloon catheters, cell salvage, massive transfusion protocol standby, and midline incision
Elective caesarean delivery at 39 weeks under the care of a general obstetrician, as the accreta is confined to the uterus without bladder involvement
Plan vaginal delivery with active third-stage management and immediate availability of an operating theatre for any postpartum haemorrhage emergency
Schedule delivery at 34 weeks to minimise spontaneous labour risk, then perform primary caesarean hysterectomy without any attempted uterine conservation
Admit at 34 weeks for continuous inpatient monitoring and deliver only if spontaneous labour occurs or foetal distress develops on CTG
Defer all corticosteroid therapy until the temporal artery biopsy result returns for confirmation
Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily and outpatient temporal artery biopsy in 6 weeks
IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg with biopsy within 1-2 weeks
Oral aspirin 100 mg daily and clinical observation without commencing any corticosteroid therapy
MRI of the head with contrast as the definitive diagnostic test prior to any steroid
Reassure that endometriosis is confirmed on ultrasound and commence the COCP continuously without further investigation
Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine device now and review her symptoms in 6 months
Start a GnRH agonist with add-back tibolone for 6 months and reassess symptoms
Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without prior imaging or specialist multidisciplinary review at all
Refer to a multidisciplinary endometriosis service, arrange MRI for deep infiltrating disease, and address fertility
Folic acid 0.5 mg orally daily for 3 months pre-conception and the first trimester only
Iodine 150 microg daily plus vitamin D level testing only without any medication change overall
Reassure her that no change to current AED therapy is needed because she is presently seizure-free
Administer MMR and varicella booster immunisations now before any pregnancy attempt is made by this patient
Refer to neurology to switch to a safer anti-seizure medication and prescribe folic acid 5 mg daily
Parental peripheral blood karyotype testing as the very first single investigation in this workup
Inherited thrombophilia screen including factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, protein C, protein S, antithrombin
Diagnostic hysteroscopy with directed biopsies regardless of any prior pelvic ultrasound findings reported recently
Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies repeated on two occasions 12 weeks apart
Peripheral blood natural killer cell assay as the very first single investigation here
Aspirin 100 mg orally once daily as sole long-term antithrombotic therapy in this patient
No antithrombotic therapy at all; lifestyle modification only with cardiovascular risk factor optimisation
Warfarin titrated to INR target 2.0 to 3.0, with weekly point-of-care monitoring initially
Apixaban 5 mg twice daily as standard non-valvular AF dose, with annual renal review and bleeding reassessment
Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel as a long-term stroke-prevention strategy instead of anticoagulation
Discharge with reassurance, advise hydration and counterpressure manoeuvres for presumed vasovagal syncope
Arrange urgent outpatient CT brain and EEG for suspected new-onset seizure disorder
Admit for telemetry, transthoracic echocardiogram, and inpatient cardiology review; advise driving restriction
Arrange outpatient 24-hour Holter monitor and tilt-table testing with autonomic function assessment
Commence midodrine 2.5 mg three times daily for presumed orthostatic intolerance with reassessment
Commence IV oxytocin at 1-2 mU/min, titrated every 30 minutes to 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes
Perform an artificial rupture of membranes and reassess in 4 hours before considering oxytocin
Proceed to a category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress in labour now
Site an epidural and re-examine her in 6 hours without commencing any augmentation now
Commence IV oxytocin starting at 6 mU/min with no titration interval shorter than 60 minutes
Kielland's rotational forceps for a deep transverse arrest under regional anaesthesia
Low-cavity non-rotational outlet forceps (Neville-Barnes or Simpson) under regional anaesthesia
Sequential ventouse then forceps if the initial vacuum extraction attempt fails to descend
Kiwi OmniCup soft ventouse with traction synchronous with maternal contractions only and pulls
Proceed directly to a category 2 caesarean section now without attempted instrumental delivery
Perform an artificial rupture of membranes immediately and then commence an IV oxytocin infusion
Perform a membrane sweep at the bedside today and reassess her clinically in 24 hours
Administer a third dose of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel today on the antenatal ward
Proceed directly to a category 4 caesarean section for failed induction of labour now
Insert a transcervical Foley catheter (16 Fr) with balloon inflated to 30-60 mL for 12-24 hours
Fluid restriction to 800 mL per day with daily sodium monitoring on the ward
IV 0.9% sodium chloride 125 mL/hour with oral salt, escalating to 3% saline if sodium fails to rise
IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally given today with hourly sodium monitoring on the ward
IV 3 percent hypertonic saline 150 mL bolus over 10 minutes only, with no further fluid
IV 5 percent dextrose at 100 mL/hour to reduce osmotic stress on the brain
Serum urate above the trimester-specific reference range value in isolation alone here
Hyperreflexia on clinical examination without any ankle clonus present on today's review
Platelet count 92 x10^9/L with transaminases above twice the upper limit of normal
New right upper quadrant pain in clinical isolation without any other PET features
Urine protein/creatinine ratio of 18 mg/mmol on a spot midstream sample today
Spiramycin 1 g TDS, switching to sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid only if amniocentesis confirms foetal infection
Sulphadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately for all women regardless of foetal status
Co-trimoxazole oral monotherapy without folinic acid or any foetal assessment as the definitive treatment
Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily as definitive monotherapy without spiramycin, folinic acid or referral
Termination of pregnancy without treatment given certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis at this gestation
Continue maintenance inhaled corticosteroid but withhold all systemic corticosteroids in pregnancy due to perceived foetal teratogenic risk
Nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium if severe, continue ICS, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg for 5-10 days, plus controlled oxygen
Switch the patient to a leukotriene receptor antagonist (oral montelukast) alone as monotherapy maintenance throughout pregnancy
Intravenous magnesium sulphate 2 g over 20 minutes given as the immediate first-line therapy in pregnancy
Discharge home with a short-acting beta-2 agonist alone once PEFR has reached 60% of personal best
Continue sodium valproate, commence folate 0.5 mg daily, with no need for AED drug levels during pregnancy
Switch to phenytoin before pregnancy as it carries the lowest teratogenicity profile of all the AEDs available
Switch (via neurology) from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam pre-conception, folate 5 mg daily, with therapeutic AED level monitoring
Continue valproate, add lamotrigine to permit valproate dose reduction, and use folate 0.5 mg daily throughout
Stop all antiepileptic medications before conception entirely to eliminate teratogenic risk to the developing foetus completely
Continue 100 microg daily, recheck TSH at 28 weeks gestation, target TSH <4.0 mIU/L throughout the entire pregnancy
Reduce levothyroxine by 25% because pregnancy is associated with a hyperthyroid physiological state secondary to beta-hCG cross-reactivity
Cease levothyroxine completely now to assess underlying endogenous thyroid function before any further dose adjustment is made in pregnancy
Increase levothyroxine 25-30% now (e.g. 125 microg daily), check TSH 4-weekly until 20 weeks, with trimester-specific target
Increase dose by 25-30% but only check TSH if she develops overt clinical symptoms of hypothyroidism in this pregnancy
Oral cyanocobalamin 1000 micrograms daily indefinitely and review symptoms in clinic in 4 weeks
Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM on alternate days until improvement plateaus, then 1 mg every 2 months
Start oral folic acid 5 mg daily while awaiting the Schilling test or holotranscobalamin level
Cease the metformin and recheck the B12 level at routine follow-up in 3 months
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells today given the new neurological symptoms and anaemia
Stop heparin and observe expectantly; restart the heparin infusion once platelets have recovered fully
Continue enoxaparin at therapeutic dose and add aspirin 100 mg daily for additional antiplatelet cover
Start warfarin immediately to bridge through the heparin withdrawal phase with target INR 2-3
Transfuse platelets to correct the thrombocytopenia and reduce the risk of major spontaneous haemorrhage
Stop all heparin AND start a non-heparin anticoagulant (fondaparinux or argatroban) at therapeutic dose
Discharge home with routine outpatient haematology referral in 2 weeks for further workup
Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe in ED for 24 hours before specialist referral
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells and 1 pool of platelets then reassess clinically
Same-day transfer to haematology for urgent bone marrow biopsy with empirical ATRA if APML suspected
Repeat the full blood count in 48 hours to confirm the pancytopenia before any specialist referral
Biopsy showing CD30+ CD15+ CD20- Reed-Sternberg cells in mixed inflammatory infiltrate
Reproducible alcohol-induced pain in the affected lymph node region after ingestion
Drenching night sweats with documented unintentional weight loss exceeding 10 percent baseline
Painless rubbery supraclavicular lymph node confirmed enlarged on bedside clinical examination
PET-CT showing supradiaphragmatic-only nodal involvement consistent with early-stage disease distribution
Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV daily plus IV hydration 3 L/m^2/day with close electrolyte monitoring
Allopurinol 300 mg daily and IV fluid hydration 2 L/m^2/day, without any rasburicase use
Urinary alkalinisation with intravenous sodium bicarbonate infusion targeting urine pH above 7.5 consistently
Delay chemotherapy by 7 days to allow oral allopurinol to reduce baseline serum urate substantially
Prophylactic upfront haemodialysis arranged via nephrology prior to commencing the first chemotherapy cycle
Plain spine X-rays in the emergency department and outpatient MRI booked within 1 week
Dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat AND urgent MRI of the whole spine within 24 hours
Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis to rule out an infective transverse myelopathy first
Commence opioid analgesia with regular paracetamol and review symptoms in clinic next week
Whole-body bone scan to identify the lesion site before any definitive further management
IV 0.9% sodium chloride 200-300 mL/hr titrated to urine output, then zoledronic acid 4 mg once euvolaemic
IV furosemide 80 mg before adequate fluid resuscitation to enhance urinary calcium excretion early
Subcutaneous calcitonin as sole monotherapy without bisphosphonate or any IV fluid resuscitation at all
Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily without intravenous fluids or bisphosphonate therapy as first-line treatment
Haemodialysis arranged as the first-line treatment regardless of underlying renal function or volume status
IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL (or calcium chloride 10% 10 mL via central access)
IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct the metabolic acidosis and stabilise the membrane
Insulin 10 units IV with 50 mL of 50% dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly now
Stop the massive transfusion protocol immediately pending haemodynamic improvement and clinical reassessment now
IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes to reduce ongoing surgical site fibrinolytic bleeding
Reassure and recheck FSH in 6 months as a single elevated FSH is non-diagnostic alone
Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill solely for menopausal symptom control without further workup needed
Bone density scan and fertility counselling referral, but defer hormonal therapy until age 40 years
Refer to endocrinology for an adrenal failure work-up before initiating any hormonal therapy in her
Systemic HRT, fertility counselling, screen for adrenal/thyroid antibodies and karyotype, plus baseline bone densitometry
Avoid all oestrogen-containing therapies indefinitely and continue non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers and lubricants only
Commence systemic oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h plus an LNG-IUS for endometrial protection
Trial low-dose vaginal oestriol cream after discussion with breast oncologist, continuing non-hormonal moisturisers
Refer for fractional CO2 vaginal laser treatment as first-line alternative in this patient
Commence ospemifene orally as the first-line therapy for her dyspareunia in this setting
